coredata.dc:title: Analytical modeling of photon absorption coefficient in mono and bilayer circular graphene quantum dots for light absorber applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Tamandani, Shahryar
Darvish, Ghafar
coredata.dc:description: We present an analytical method to calculate photon absorption coefficient in mono and bilayer circular graphene quantum dots (CGQDs). We use kobo equation to extract new closed relation as the main goal. First, we calculate real and imaginary part of optical conductance separately. Then, joint density of states is obtained using a new relation that was extracted for the energy levels of mono and bilayer circular grapheme quantum dots. In this work we use closed equations to calculate energy levels in CGQDs. Next we obtain a new closed formula to calculate the photon absorption coefficient. The results show that the absorption coefficient is related to the size of CGQDs and number of layers. The photon absorption coefficient becomes lower with larger size of CGQDs. It is seen that the results of our method is compatible with the results of practical works. We also compare photon absorption in biased and unbiased bilayer CGQDs and investigate the effect of external magnetic field on photon absorption. © 2001 Elsevier Science. All rights reserved
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Dirac equation
Energy levels
Absorption coefficient

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical conduction in mono layer graphene
2.4. Effect of second nearest neighbors on the conductance of GQD

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Atomic structure of monolayer graphene quantum dots.
Energy levels of a mono layer circular graphene quantum dot under a perpendicular magnetic field. The energy spectra at the K and K′ valleys are shown by the dashed and solid lines, respectively.
Energy levels of bilayer CGQD versus QD radius under various bias conditions.
Real part of optical conductance of a monolayer CGQDs corresponding to the first four energy levels for various radii of CGQDs.
Absorption coefficient as a function of energy for a monolayer CGQDs with different radii of QDs.
Absorption coefficient of a monolayer CGQDs with r=8nm for various magnetic field..
Absorption coefficient as a function of energy for a bilayer CGQDs with different radii of QDs.
Absorption coefficient of a bilayer CGQDs under various bias conditions for r=8nm.

head.ce:title: Analytical modeling of photon absorption coefficient in mono and bilayer circular graphene quantum dots for light absorber applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical Engineering, Science and Research Branch, Islamic Azad University, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electrical Engineering, Science and Research Branch, Islamic Azad University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We present an analytical method to calculate photon absorption coefficient in mono and bilayer circular graphene quantum dots (CGQDs). We use kobo equation to extract new closed relation as the main goal. First, we calculate real and imaginary part of optical conductance separately. Then, joint density of states is obtained using a new relation that was extracted for the energy levels of mono and bilayer circular grapheme quantum dots. In this work we use closed equations to calculate energy levels in CGQDs. Next we obtain a new closed formula to calculate the photon absorption coefficient. The results show that the absorption coefficient is related to the size of CGQDs and number of layers. The photon absorption coefficient becomes lower with larger size of CGQDs. It is seen that the results of our method is compatible with the results of practical works. We also compare photon absorption in biased and unbiased bilayer CGQDs and investigate the effect of external magnetic field on photon absorption. © 2001 Elsevier Science. All rights reserved

ce:section.ce:para: Light (Optical photons) absorption is one of the parameters that have a major effect on optical device characteristic like solar cells efficiency. In materials with bulk structure or even with thin, layers light absorption is the most important parameter. Classical models like Drude or Drude-Lorenz describe absorption coefficient based on dielectric function [1]. This model is effective especially when photon absorption is done by electrons of valence and/or conduction band. But, light absorption in nano-structure field leads to excitons formation which has a crucial role in finding the absorption coefficient. Therefore, this coefficient must be calculated by quantum and non-classic methods.

In 2008, Stauber et al. [1] tried to calculate graphene optical conduction. Their proposed model did not mention quantum dots directly but its results can be used for analyses. In 2011, Ahmadi et al. conducted photon absorption process on graphene nano-ribbons. Their results were highly consistent with actual values [2]. Their idea was proper but didn’t calculate photon absorption coefficient. In 2008, Zhang et al. tried to obtain electronic and optical properties of hexagonal quantum dots with zigzag and armchair boundary conditions using tight binding method. Results gained from the monolayer quantum dots and effects of magnetic field have been evaluated for those properties [3]. This team had no research on circular and bilayer quantum dots. In 2011 Grujic et al. presented an analytical solution to calculate energy levels and light absorption in monolayer circular quantum dots in presence of magnetic field, and compared the results with those of tight binding method [4]. This research was also conducted on monolayer quantum dots, and effect of multi-layered quantum dots on parameters has not been investigated analytically. In 2011, Zarenia et al. studied triangular and hexagonal quantum dots, and calculated energy levels of quantum dots in presence of magnetic field [5]. In their study neither bilayer quantum dots nor circular quantum dots have been studied. In 2014, Costa et al. conducted a numerical analysis on bilayer quantum dots to obtain energy levels and wave function with/without the presence of magnetic field [6]. Their research didn’t lead to closed relations, but they obtained wave functions and energy levels by numerical methods. In 2015, Costa et al. conducted an analytical study on energy levels of triangular and hexagonal bilayer quantum dots [7]. They worked on AA and AB stacked structures and compared their results with tight binding simulation. In their work, energy levels correlation to quantum dots dimension is evaluated. They did not, however, investigate circular dots. Their calculations were based on numerical methods. Here we intended to find an appropriate method to calculate absorption coefficient of graphene quantum dots. We focus on circular CGQDs and use analytical method to calculate energy levels and light absorption coefficient.

Organization of this paper is as follows: in Section 2, we present the theoretical background and analytical expressions for CGQDs. Then, a new formulation for calculation of absorption coefficient is proposed. In 2.2Section 3, we discuss and compare the analytical results for the mono and bilayer CGQDs and then analytically investigate the effect of external magnetic field on photon absorption in CGQDs. Finally, we conclude this paper in Section 4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical conduction in mono layer graphene
ce:section.ce:para:

Then, we investigate dependence of the real part of the optical conductance on quantum dots radius. Using the optical conduction of the CGQDs, we can calculate the absorption coefficient of CGQDs. From the imaginary part of dielectric function or the real part of the optical conductance, the absorption coefficient can be calculated by using the following relation [1]:
(22)
α
d
=
2 ω
ϵ
2
/ 2
C
=
2

p
2
τ ω
C
2

8 π
δ
DC
ω
C
2
where
δ
DC
= ℜ
δ
xx
[1]. It is clear that any change in the radius of quantum dots will change the absorption coefficient.

Using an approach based on the Dirac–Weyl equation, we analytically calculate the energy levels of a CGQD under a perpendicular magnetic field with Infinite Mass Boundary Condition (IMBC). The Dirac–Weyl Hamiltonian, which describes the low-energy states in the vicinity of the K point in the presence of an external magnetic field and an electrostatic potential V(r), is given by:
(23)
H =
υ
F
( P + e A ) . σ + τ V
( r )
σ
z
where P is the momentum operator, A is the vector potential of the applied magnetic field B, and
σ = (
σ
x
,
σ
y
)
are the Pauli matrices which take into account the contributions of two different graphene sublattices. The potential is assumed to be circular symmetric with radius R (i.e. V(r)=0 for r<R and
V ( r ) → ∞
for
r ≥ R
, where r denotes the radial coordinate and R is QD radius). To simplify the calculations, we use the dimensionless variables
ρ = r / R
,
β =
R
2

2

K ′

should be replaced with
σ
*
. The Dirac–Weyl equation for the states in both the K and

is the regularized confluent hyper geometric function and C is the normalization constant. Similarly, we can calculate

. Using Eq. (30), the eigenvalue equation for the IMBC is given by:
(31)
τ ε

ε
2
4 β
, m + 1 , β ) = 0

Now, using Eq. (31), we can obtain energy levels of CGQD with applied different magnetic field and then calculate absorption coefficient. Fig. 2 illustrates the five lowest energy levels of a CGQD with radius of r=60nm for different m in the range of −4 to 4 under a perpendicular magnetic field with the IMBC. As already stated, the use of IMBC is justified by the existence of the magnetization related confinement [4], which leads to the energy gap in the energy spectrum, as observed in Fig. 2. The magnetic ordering at the QD edge breaks reversal symmetry, and thus the electron-hole symmetry for each valley is broken even when an external magnetic field is absent. Therefore, the energies of the electron and hole states in a given valley are not mutually related to each other. However, the magnetic ordering cannot break the intervalley electron-hole symmetry when the IMBC is considered and determined by the
ε
K
= −
ε
K ′
. When the magnetic field increases, the QD states merge to form the Landau levels (LL) of graphene. Here the Landau level index is denoted by n. For the IMBC, the first LL (n=1) is composed out of m≤0 states, and the higher energy (n>1) LLs are formed out of m<n states in both the K and K′ valleys. Such a behavior is similar to semiconductor QD's [4]..

Furthermore, two different regimes of carrier confinement should be considered. At low magnetic fields, the carrier confinement is due to graphene termination (edge confinement). When the magnetic field is large, the effect of the edge is suppressed and the carrier confinement is affected by magnetic field. Therefore, the transition between the two confinement regimes takes place with increasing the magnetic field, as shown in Fig. 2. The transition points can be defined as the points where the energies of the states in the QD differ negligibly from the LL energy. When |m| increases, these transition points occur at a larger magnetic field.

The solutions of Eqs. (30) and (31) are used in the calculation of the optical absorption. The matrix elements of the transition from the initial state i to the final state f are given by
M
f
i
= 〈
Ψ
f
| r |
Ψ
i
〉
, where light is assumed to be polarized in the plane of the dot and r is amplitude of light. The absorption can be obtained by adding all possible transitions,
A =
∑
i , f
A
i
f
, where A
i f is determined by assuming elastic broadening of the absorption line, as described by the Lorentzian [4]:
(32)
A
i
f
=
(
f
F D
(
E
i
,
E
F
, T ) -
f
F D
(

, T )
)
× δ ( ℏ ω − (
E

−
E
i
) )
|
M
f
i
|
2

Because of axial symmetry, it is straightforward to derive the selection rule for the optical transitions when m
f =m
i . Therefore, transitions between states with the same total angular momentum number are only allowed. There is no restriction on the principal quantum number, which is in contrast to the case of bulk graphene, where transitions between adjacent LLs are only allowed [4].

Bilayer CGQDs are another form of CGQDs, which we calculate their absorption coefficient as well. To do so, the following relation can be used [1]:
(33)
α

where
t
⊥
is the hopping amplitude between the graphene planes and its value is about 0.4eV. For photons with energy much larger than
t
⊥
, which is the case of the visible region, we can obtain:
(36)

2

≃ 1 −

)
2
≃ 1

Then, we can calculate the absorption coefficient for bilayer CGQDs by calculating the energy levels of bilayer CGQDs. In order to calculate energy levels in bilayer graphene (BLG), we employ the tight binding approach within the nearest-neighbor approximation. The Hamiltonian for BLG reads [6,7]:
(37)
H
T B
=
∑
i ≠ j
(
t
⊥
c
i
†
c
j
+ h . c . )
+
∑ i
V
i
c
i
†
c
i
where
c
i
and
c
i
†
are annihilation and creation ratios of an electron in site i, respectively. V
i is the on-site potential, which we consider V
i =V for the atoms in the upper layer and V
i =−V for the atoms in the lower layer in the case of biased bilayer CGQDs. Diagonalizing the matrix form of the Hamiltonian H TB, we can obtain the energy levels of bilayer CGQDs [7]. Fig. 3 shows energy levels in bilayer CGQDs. Results are shown for V=0 and V=0.1eV..

At zero bias, the energy levels decrease with increasing radius of the QD because the lowest energy bands in BLG follow the dispersion relation
(38)
E ≈ ±
[
V

From this equation, we see that
E ∝ ±
1 / r
for V=0 and large r, whereas with applying bias voltage and for large r,
E ≈ ± [ V − 2 n π ℏ
υ
F
/
r
2
]
which shows size dependence of the energy levels of the bilayer CGQDs. For this reason, the lowest electron and hole states in the spectrum of Fig. 3 approach the energies lower than the applied potential V in large sizes of the GQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 2.4. Effect of second nearest neighbors on the conductance of GQD
ce:section.ce:para:
To obtain the conductivity of graphene, we must rearrange the Green's function versus t′, as follows:
(39)
G

) =

(40)

)

From Eq. (40), one can see that only poles are modified compared with Eq. (13) and the coherence factors have the same form as in the case of t′=0. The current operator, which is derived from the tight-binding Hamiltonian, can be written in momentum space as:
(41)
j
x

=
j

+

(42)

(43)

3 t '

The operators
j
x
,
t
P
and
j
x
,
t '
P
are the current operators associated with the hopping amplitudes t and t′, respectively. Substituting Eqs. (42) and (43) into Eq. (10), the current-current correlation function is obtained, which consists of three different terms. These terms are related to two
j
x
,
t
P
,
j
x
,
t
P
and
j
x
,
t '
P
, and two
j
x
,
t '
P
operators, respectively. The last term is zero, since it corresponds to the current-current correlation function of a triangular lattice. By doing a local gauge transformation to the fermionic operators of one sub-lattice, the crossed term becomes zero as well. The first term has the same form of Eq. (19), and is written as:
(44)
ℜ
δ
xx
=
δ
0
π

)

)

18 −

)

t a n h
ℏ ω + 2

/ ( 4

2
) - 3 ] + 2 μ
4

B
T
+ t a n h

t ′

2
/ ( 4
t
2
) - 3 ] − 2 μ
4
k
B
T
]
Therefore, the hopping amplitude t′ affects the conductivity of graphene via only the band structure E± in the Fermi functions.

Using Eq. (19), we calculate the real part of the optical conductance for mono layer CGQDs, which is shown in Fig. 4. The obtained results show that the absorption coefficient has discrete values due to the discrete CGQDs density of states. Typical CGQDs radii are in the range of 2–15nm, which is in agreement with the experimental data [7–9]..
As illustrated in this figure, the real part of optical conductance shifts toward the lower energy as the radius of QDs increases due to decreasing bandgap energy of QDs.
Using this parameter and Eq. (21), the photon absorption coefficient of the monolayer CGQDs can be obtained, as illustrated in Fig. 5..

Comparing these results with those reported in [9], we can see that our results are in line with the experimental results. The proposed formalism can be used to calculate the absorption in CGQDs based solar cell like that presented in [9].

It is found that with the increase of the CGQD's radius, there is a red shift for the absorption peak. The reason for this is that the differences between energy levels become smaller when R (radius of CGQDs) increases due to quantum size effect [17]. As the CGQDs become lager, the density of state increases. Therefore, more transitions can occur. This characteristic is consistent with the case of conventional semiconductor QDs [17].

Furthermore, we investigate the absorption coefficient of CGQDs under external magnetic field. The interband absorption are calculated at T=300K. As mentioned previously, optical transitions between non-adjacent LLs in a graphene sheet are not allowed. On the other hand, selection rule is not applied to n. This is in contrast to the case of massive graphene in which the transitions are allowed only between adjacent Landau levels [4]. Although transitions between states which do not differ by ±1 in the value of n are allowed in the GQD, their contribution to the overall absorption is very much smaller than the contribution of the n→n±1 transitions. To obtain the absorption spectra, the matrix elements between the five lowest energy states for m in the range −4 to +4 are taken into account.
Fig. 6 illustrates the absorption coefficients of a monolayer CGQDs for various values of the magnetic field..

The absorption spectra exhibit narrow absorption lines. These absorption lines are created by the n=−1→n=0 and n=0→n=1 transitions in the case of the IMBC. For this case, the energies of the transitions between the states in the two valleys are equal. This is the reason why these absorption lines appear.

Next, we calculate the absorption coefficient of bilayer CGQDs. Fig. 7 indicates the absorption coefficient of bilayer CGQDs with different radii of QDs. As observed from this figure, the absorption in bilayer CGQDs shifts toward lower energy with increasing the radius of QDs due to the decrease of the bandgap energy of QDs..
Comparison of absorption coefficients between the unbiased and biased bilayer CGQDs is shown in Fig. 8..

As one can see from Fig. 8, in the biased condition, change in intraband transitions (lower energies) is negligible. In this case, difference between energy levels in conduction and valance bands is similar to unbiased condition because the term
n π ℏ
υ
F
/ r in Eq. (38) is dominant. However, we can see the significant difference in interband transitions (higher energies) between two bias conditions. Because of increasing band gap in biased condition, the interband transitions blue shift to higher energies.

In this paper, we have proposed a new formulation for the calculation of photon absorption coefficient in multilayer CGQDs. Using the presented formalism, we have obtained the real and imaginary parts of the optical conductance and then analytically calculated the absorption coefficient using the real part of the optical conductance. The results show that these parameters are related to the radius of QDs and the number of CGQDs layers.

As represented, the absorption peaks shift to the lower energy (red shift) with the increase the radius of QDs. Furthermore, we have demonstrated that the absorption coefficient peaks of the bilayer CGQDs are in the higher energy in comparison with those of the monolayer CGQDs. The reason lies in the different bandgap energies. The absorption coefficient takes on its maximum value at the photon energy equal to the bandgap energy, and then it decreases with increasing the photon energy until the energy of photon reaches the second energy gap belongs to another sub band transition and subsequently the absorption coefficient again increases. Therefore, we can suitably tune the absorption coefficient to a desired energy by choosing a proper shape of CGQDs. We have also demonstrated that the effect of the edge on the electron confinement can be suppressed by increasing the magnetic field due to merging the states into Landau levels.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: E. Ahmadi, A. Asgari, Proceedings of the 2nd International Science, Social-Science, Engineering and Energy Conference Engineering Science and Management, 2010, http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.proeng.2011.03.005.
Peng Xin-Xiang, Liao Wen-Ru, Zhou Guang-Hui, vol. 25(9), 2008, p. 3436. http://dx.doi.org/10.1088/0256-307X/25/9/092.
